subtopic_detail: Acute otitis media without severe features in a well child over 24 months
stem: A 26-month-old child presents with 24 hours of left ear pain and unsettled sleep, with a temperature of 38.2 degrees Celsius. He is feeding well and is alert. There is no history of recurrent ear infection and no perforation or otorrhoea. Father asks whether antibiotics are needed today.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, RR 24, T 38.2, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mildly bulging erythematous left tympanic membrane with intact light reflex; right ear normal; no mastoid tenderness; no rash

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days starting at this visit today
options.rationale: First-line antibiotics are reserved for under 6 months, severe systemic illness, perforation with discharge, bilateral disease in under 2 year olds, or failure of watchful waiting at 48 hours. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg once daily for 3 days starting at this visit today
options.rationale: Macrolides are not first-line for acute otitis media in Australia; if antibiotics are indicated, amoxicillin is preferred. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: acute otitis media)

options.text: Regular paracetamol with safety-netting and planned clinical review at 48 hours
options.rationale: AU practice supports a watch and wait approach with adequate analgesia for non-severe acute otitis media in children over 24 months without high-risk features. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Otitis Media)

options.text: Same-day ENT referral for myringotomy with grommet insertion under general anaesthetic
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for recurrent or chronic otitis media with effusion and hearing impact, not an uncomplicated first presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute Otitis Media)

options.text: Topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops twice daily for 7 days with home review
options.rationale: Topical ear antibiotics treat otitis externa or chronic suppurative otitis media with perforation, not acute otitis media with an intact tympanic membrane. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: acute otitis media)

explanation.summary: Australian practice supports watchful waiting with analgesia for non-severe acute otitis media in children over 24 months who are systemically well. Antibiotics are reserved for children under 6 months, severe systemic illness, perforation with otorrhoea, bilateral disease in under 2 year olds, or failure to improve at 48 hours. First-line if needed is amoxicillin; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or cefuroxime is used in true penicillin allergy. Recurrent acute otitis media (three episodes in 6 months or four in 12 months) prompts ENT referral for grommet assessment.
key_points: Watch and wait with analgesia suits non-severe acute otitis media in children over 24 months
Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is first-line when antibiotics are indicated
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children, infants under 6 months, and severe illness skip the wait
explanation.pearls: Adequate paracetamol 15 mg/kg every 4 to 6 hours combined with ibuprofen 10 mg/kg every 6 to 8 hours controls otalgia in the first 48 hours better than antibiotics.

subtopic_detail: Mild to moderate croup disposition and treatment selection
stem: A 2-year-old boy presents in the early hours of the morning with a 1 day coryzal illness followed by a sudden onset barking cough and noisy breathing. He has a mild intermittent inspiratory stridor at rest, mild chest wall recession on excitement, and settles when held by his mother. He is taking oral fluids. There are no drooling, dysphagia, or unvaccinated immunisation status concerns.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 97% room air, T 37.8
data_table.Examination: Hoarse voice, barking cough, mild inspiratory stridor at rest, mild recession on crying, equal air entry, no foreign body history

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Empirical oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for suspected bacterial tracheitis with a chest X-ray to confirm
options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis presents with high fever, toxicity and copious tracheal secretions; this child has a typical croup pattern and antibiotics are not indicated. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL and admit to the paediatric high dependency unit overnight for continuous monitoring of airway
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe or life-threatening croup with persistent stridor at rest; this child has mild croup that settles when held. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Croup)

options.text: Single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, observe until stridor at rest settles and oral intake is reliable, then discharge with safety-net advice
options.rationale: The RCH Melbourne Croup CPG uses oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for mild or moderate croup, reserving 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg) for severe or life-threatening disease. Post-dexamethasone observation is tied to clinical resolution rather than a fixed time clock; the 3-hour rule applies after nebulised adrenaline only. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Croup)

options.text: Cool mist humidification therapy and discharge home with parental reassurance and no medication for this distressing nocturnal symptom
options.rationale: Humidification has not been shown to alter the course of croup; the RCH CPG recommends oral dexamethasone for all severities including mild. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Croup)

options.text: Urgent ENT review with anaesthetic team on standby for fibreoptic laryngoscopy to exclude any structural airway pathology
options.rationale: Airway instrumentation risks complete obstruction and is not indicated for typical viral croup; the RCH pathway uses oral dexamethasone alone for mild disease. (Source: Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: The RCH Melbourne Croup CPG uses oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg for mild or moderate croup; 0.6 mg/kg (max 12 mg) is reserved for severe or life-threatening disease where nebulised adrenaline is also given. Post-dexamethasone observation is tied to clinical resolution (stridor at rest settled, oral intake reliable) rather than a fixed time clock; the 3-hour observation rule applies after nebulised adrenaline only.
key_points: RCH dex dose: 0.15 mg/kg for mild/moderate; 0.6 mg/kg for severe/life-threatening only
Post-dex observation tied to clinical resolution, not a fixed 4-hour wait
3-hour rule applies after nebulised adrenaline only
Nebulised adrenaline reserved for severe or life-threatening croup
explanation.pearls: The dexamethasone peak effect is at 1 to 2 hours; reassess airway and feeding before discharge.

subtopic_detail: Acute asthma exacerbation in a 5 year old: oral prednisolone course
stem: A 5-year-old girl with a known diagnosis of asthma presents with 2 days of cough and wheeze after a viral upper respiratory tract infection. She has used her salbutamol metered dose inhaler with spacer 4 hourly at home without sustained relief. She is talking in short phrases, with mild intercostal recession. She has been given salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer in the department with a partial response.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 38, SpO2 94% room air, T 37.2
data_table.Examination: Bilateral expiratory wheeze with prolonged expiratory phase, mild subcostal recession, no cyanosis, alert

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in addition to ongoing bronchodilator therapy?

options.text: Nebulised ipratropium bromide 250 micrograms every 20 minutes for the first hour
options.rationale: Ipratropium is added to severe exacerbations in the first hour, not as the next single step in a moderate exacerbation with a partial salbutamol response. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg as a single dose given over 20 minutes now
options.rationale: Intravenous magnesium is reserved for severe asthma not responding to inhaled bronchodilators and oral steroids. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Inhaled fluticasone propionate 125 micrograms twice daily as preventer initiation
options.rationale: Preventer initiation is a planned outpatient decision; it does not treat the acute exacerbation. (Source: National Asthma Council: Australian Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council: Australian Asthma Handbook

options.text: Empirical oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days as adjunctive cover
options.rationale: Most paediatric asthma exacerbations are viral and antibiotics offer no benefit without focal pneumonia or signs of bacterial infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 3 days starting at this presentation
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days is standard AU practice for acute asthma exacerbations of moderate or greater severity in children. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Acute asthma in school-aged children is graded mild, moderate, severe, or critical based on speech, work of breathing, oxygen saturation, and conscious state. Salbutamol via spacer is the cornerstone of bronchodilator delivery in children. Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days is added in moderate or greater exacerbations and reduces relapse. Severe disease may require ipratropium, intravenous magnesium sulfate, intravenous aminophylline, or intravenous salbutamol with paediatric intensive care input.
key_points: Salbutamol via spacer beats nebuliser for delivery and side effects in mild to moderate disease
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days suits moderate exacerbations from age 1 year
Intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes is reserved for severe disease
explanation.pearls: Spacer delivered salbutamol gives 6 puffs for a child under 6 years and 12 puffs for older children, repeated every 20 minutes for the first hour in moderate exacerbations.

sources.label: National Asthma Council: Australian Asthma Handbook

subtopic_detail: First febrile UTI in young girl: imaging algorithm
stem: An 18-month-old girl is admitted with a first episode of fever to 39 degrees Celsius and vomiting. A clean catch urine culture grows greater than 10 to the 8 colony forming units per litre of Escherichia coli sensitive to trimethoprim. She responds to intravenous gentamicin with defervescence by 36 hours and switches to oral trimethoprim to complete a 7 day course. Family history is negative for renal disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 130, RR 30, T 38.4 on day 2, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Well perfused, soft non-tender abdomen, no flank mass, normal external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: CRP peak 96 mg/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, urine culture E. coli >10^8 CFU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate further imaging plan after clinical recovery?

options.text: Urgent micturating cystourethrogram during the admission to exclude grade V vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: Routine MCUG is no longer recommended after a single uncomplicated UTI; it is selected for atypical illness or abnormal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks of the acute illness with further imaging guided by findings
options.rationale: AU practice for a first febrile UTI in a child under 2 years is a renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks, with MCUG or DMSA reserved for atypical features or abnormal ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: DMSA renal scintigraphy at 6 weeks regardless of ultrasound findings to look for cortical scarring
options.rationale: DMSA is reserved for recurrent or atypical UTI or abnormal ultrasound, not routine for a single uncomplicated infection. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast to map the urinary tract
options.rationale: CT involves unnecessary radiation in young children and is not part of routine UTI follow up. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

options.text: No imaging is required; arrange continuous low dose trimethoprim prophylaxis for 12 months
options.rationale: Routine antibiotic prophylaxis without imaging is no longer recommended after a single uncomplicated febrile UTI. (Source: RCH CPG: Urinary tract infection)

explanation.summary: Australian paediatric guidance for a first febrile urinary tract infection in a child under 2 years uses renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks as the screening investigation, looking for hydronephrosis, posterior urethral valves, duplex systems, or other structural abnormality. MCUG and DMSA are second-line and reserved for atypical UTI (non-Escherichia coli organism, septic illness, failure to respond within 48 hours, or abnormal voiding) or an abnormal ultrasound. Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is no longer recommended in the absence of high-grade reflux or other anatomical risk.
key_points: Renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks is first-line after a first febrile UTI in a child under 2 years
Atypical illness or abnormal ultrasound triggers MCUG or DMSA
Routine antibiotic prophylaxis after a single uncomplicated UTI is not supported by current evidence
explanation.pearls: An atypical UTI is one with seriously ill child, poor urine flow, abdominal mass, raised creatinine, septicaemia, non-Escherichia coli organism, or failure to respond within 48 hours, and prompts additional imaging.

sources.label: NICE NG224: Urinary tract infection in under 16s

subtopic_detail: Functional constipation in a preschool child: disimpaction and maintenance
stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought in by his mother with a 4 month history of infrequent hard stools, with bowel actions every 4 to 5 days and visible straining. There is intermittent soiling between bowel actions. Toilet training started at 30 months without success. Abdominal examination reveals a palpable left iliac fossa mass that is faecal in character. Anal examination shows no fissures or perianal disease. There are no red flag features for organic disease.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, palpable faecal mass in left iliac fossa, normal external anus, no fissure, normal sacral spine
data_table.Investigations: Not indicated based on history and examination

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Plain abdominal radiograph today to quantify faecal loading and guide laxative dosing
options.rationale: Abdominal radiography correlates poorly with clinical faecal loading and adds radiation without changing management. (Source: NICE CG99: Constipation in children)

options.text: Glycerol suppository now and review the family in 6 weeks without further laxative use
options.rationale: Rectal interventions are second-line for non-responders; oral macrogol disimpaction is preferred and acceptable to children. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Disimpaction with oral macrogol over 3 to 7 days then a daily maintenance osmotic laxative
options.rationale: AU practice for functional constipation with faecal loading begins with oral macrogol disimpaction then transitions to a maintenance osmotic laxative for several months. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Referral to paediatric gastroenterology for rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease typically presents with delayed meconium, failure to thrive, and an empty rectum; this presentation is functional. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Increased dietary fibre and water intake alone with planned follow up in 3 months
options.rationale: Dietary advice alone is inadequate with established faecal loading and soiling; pharmacological disimpaction is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in preschool children commonly presents with infrequent hard stools, withholding behaviours, and overflow soiling. Australian and UK practice favours oral macrogol (polyethylene glycol) for both disimpaction and maintenance, starting with escalating doses for 3 to 7 days, then a single daily maintenance dose for several months and slow weaning. Toilet training with positive routines, soft stool maintenance, and parental education are central. Red flag features (delayed meconium, ribbon stools, perianal disease, abnormal neurology, failure to thrive) warrant gastroenterology review.
key_points: Oral macrogol disimpaction over 3 to 7 days then maintenance is first-line
Soiling between hard stools usually reflects overflow from impaction, not behaviour
Maintenance laxative is continued for months with slow weaning, not stopped at first improvement
explanation.pearls: Macrogol 3350 with electrolytes is dosed at 1 to 1.5 g/kg daily for disimpaction in divided doses and 0.4 to 0.8 g/kg daily for maintenance in preschool children.
